title: Syllabus
text: This course introduces students to quantitative research methods in international relations and European integration using modern tools for scientific programming and reproducible research. Students will learn how to analyze and visualize data in R, document their work using Quarto, and manage their projects efficiently using Git and GitHub. These tools enable students to follow state-of-the-art workflows for transparent, collaborative, and well-organized empirical research. All work will be conducted in Positron, an integrated development environment for data science.

Students will learn how to analyze and visualize data in R, document their work using Quarto, and manage their projects efficiently using Git and GitHub. title: Syllabus
text:
Course Objectives
The overall goal of the course is to equip students with the practical skills and methodological confidence necessary to write an empirical bachelor thesis in political science. Students will learn how to preprocess data, select and implement appropriate statistical models for continuous and binary outcomes, compute quantities of interest, and interpret results accurately. Beyond technical proficiency, the course emphasizes sound statistical reasoning, effective data visualization, and the clear communication of empirical findings in a way that is accessible to nontechnical audiences. Through regular assignments and a cumulative final project (take-home exam), students will develop the ability to critically evaluate statistical claims in both academic research and public discourse, providing a solid foundation for conducting their own empirical analyses.

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Logistics

General Information
Each session consists of an instructor-led lecture on concepts and theory, followed by hands-on exercises. Homework is assigned at the end of class and due one week later. Readings provide background, theory, and context for the session. Because homework deadlines are tied to class days, students should remain in their assigned group (Mon or Thu) as their main class. Attending the other day is allowed in case of sickness or other exceptions, but this should remain the exception rather than the norm.


Attendance Policy
Active participation (Mitarbeit) is a required component of the coursework (Studienleistung). This course consists of 13 class sessions. Successful participation is presumed if a student attends at least 80% of class sessions (at least 11 out of 13 sessions).
If attendance falls below 80% but remains at or above 60% (8–10 out of 13 sessions), successful participation is not presumed; in such cases, the possibility of fulfilling the participation requirement will be assessed on a case-by-case basis.
If attendance falls below 60% (fewer than 8 out of 13 sessions), successful participation is normally considered impossible under the examination regulations.
If class sessions are cancelled during the semester, the absolute number of required sessions will be adjusted accordingly while the percentage thresholds (80% and 60%) remain unchanged.


Grading Policy
According to the examination regulations, course assessment consists of coursework (Studienleistung, pass/fail) and a final examination (Prüfungsleistung, graded).
Successful completion of all coursework requirements is a prerequisite for admission to the final take-home exam.

Coursework (Studienleistung, pass/fail)
Coursework consists of two components:
1. Weekly Homework Assignments
Starting in Week 2 (no homework will be assigned in the final week of the semester), students must complete 11 weekly homework assignments. Assignments will be distributed via GitHub and assessed on a pass/fail basis.
To fulfill this requirement, students must pass at least 80% of the assignments (9 out of 11 homeworks). Homework must demonstrate a serious and complete attempt at solving the tasks. Submissions that are empty or clearly insufficient will not be counted as passed.






Note



As homework assignments only started in Week 3, the total number of assignments is 10. Consequently, students must pass at least 8 out of 10 homework assignments to be eligible to take the take-home exam.


2. Active Participation (Mitarbeit)
Active participation, as described in the Attendance Policy, is a required coursework component and will be assessed on a pass/fail basis.
Both components must be passed in order to fulfill the Studienleistung requirement.


Final Examination (Prüfungsleistung, graded)
The final examination consists of a take-home exam, currently scheduled for:
May 28, 19:00 — June 05, 23:59
The exam will be distributed via GitHub. You will receive a dataset and a problem description for a cumulative data analysis project.
You must submit a complete project repository (project folder) via GitHub. The repository must include:

Fully documented and reproducible code

A rendered PDF file presenting your results (structured similarly to a data, methods, and analysis section of a research paper)

All relevant LLM chat histories, if AI tools were used
A statutory declaration according to the University’s guidelines

The version of the repository available on GitHub at the moment the deadline passes will be evaluated.
The take-home exam will be graded according to the university grading scale (1.0 = very good to 5.0 = insufficient) and determines the overall course grade.
All tools and workflows necessary to complete the exam will be taught during the course.



Language and Software Policy
The course is taught entirely in English and focuses on using R, Quarto, and Git/GitHub for data analysis, reproducible research, and project management. Students are encouraged to work with these tools, as the course exercises and workflows are designed around them. For the take-home exam, students may use other script-based languages (e.g., Python or Stata), but this is not recommended, and no guidance will be provided for non-R workflows. The emphasis is on understanding the tools, practicing them, and learning how to independently find solutions.


Hardware
You are required to have a computer available during class. For the purposes of this course, a standard laptop is sufficient; there are no specific requirements regarding RAM, external GPUs, or other advanced hardware components.
If you do not own a suitable device, the University of Mannheim’s IT department offers a laptop rental service. Students are encouraged to make use of this option if needed.


Software
Please install all required software programs and tools following the separate installation guide before the first session, so we can address any potential issues on that day and fully begin the programming component in the subsequent lecture.


Optional Practice Materials
It is highly recommended that you take advantage of the access to DataCamp provided by this course. DataCamp is an online learning platform offering valuable opportunities to practice the material covered in this course and beyond. A good starting point is the Introduction to R tutorial. DataCamp courses are voluntary and not part of the mandatory requirements to pass the course.


Late Work Policy
Late submissions will not be accepted. If you anticipate any difficulties in meeting a deadline, you must discuss this issue with me before the deadline, not after.


AI Policy
All submissions will be checked for plagiarism, and violations of the code of conduct may result in failing the course or expulsion. While generative AI tools, such as ChatGPT or Gemini, are permitted for the final examination, any AI-generated content or code must be clearly highlighted, thoroughly commented, and accompanied by the chat history to demonstrate understanding. I will aim to occasionally provide guidance on using AI responsibly, but relying on it from the start can hinder learning: research shows AI is most effective for those who already understand the topic. For a detailed explanation of why AI can undermine learning, see this explanation by Andrew Heiss.


Mental Health Support
Life and learning at the University of Mannheim can sometimes feel complicated, challenging, or even overwhelming. This is completely normal. You are not expected to fully understand or memorize course content on the first attempt. If you ever feel mentally or emotionally strained, you may find helpful advice on the University of Mannheim’s Good Mental Health and Studying page. Additionally, the Help in Times of Crises page provides an overview of the various counseling and support services available at the university.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
QMIR 2026
Week 5: Reshaping, Merging & Data Visualization
 Tristan Muno            March 9, 2026

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 5 Learning Goals
By the end of today, you should be able to:

Use key Positron shortcuts and look up function documentation efficiently
Reshape data between wide and long format using pivot_longer() and pivot_wider()
Load and clean messy real-world Excel files using readxl and janitor
Combine datasets from multiple sources using left_join() and verify the result
Handle missing values transparently and understand the risks of imputation
Build and customise ggplot2 visualisations using aesthetics, geoms, scales, and themes
Produce publication-ready tables with tabyl(), adorn_totals(), and kable()
Embed computed results directly in prose using inline code

Knowing these will make your workflow noticeably faster.

Note


All shortcuts are fully customizable via File → Preferences → Keyboard Shortcuts. If a shortcut here does not work for you, it may already be assigned to something else – check and remap as needed. Visit this page for RStudio shortcuts in Positron.









Tip


Restart R frequently. A fresh session catches hidden dependencies between code chunks that can break rendering.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The package::function Notation
You will encounter this pattern often in documentation and Stack Overflow answers:
dplyr::filter(gapminder, year == 2007)

This is equivalent to loading the package and then calling the function:
library(dplyr)
filter(gapminder, year == 2007)



When to use it:

When two packages export a function with the same name (e.g., dplyr::filter vs stats::filter)
In scripts you share with others, to make dependencies explicit
When you only need one function from a package and don’t want to load the whole thing










Note


If you load the tidyverse with library(tidyverse), you rarely need :: notation within that ecosystem. But you will encounter it – and write it – more as your code grows.

title: QMIR 2026
section: How to Look Up a Function
text:
How to Look Up a Function


Inside Positron:

Type the function name and Positron shows a preview with argument hints
The argument you are currently typing is highlighted
help(function_name) or ?function_name opens the full documentation pane

# These are equivalent
help(pivot_longer)
?pivot_longer

Online:

Tidyverse functions have excellent reference pages with worked examples: tidyverse.org
R for Data Science (2e) is free online and is the textbook behind this course
Stack Overflow / AI for specific errors









Tip


Habit to build: Before Googling, try ?function_name first. The Examples section at the bottom of every help file is often the fastest way to understand how something works. When consulting AI, always force it to return a web source, so you can verify the output yourself!

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Note on Pipes
The pipe |> works because tidyverse functions share one convention: the data frame is always the first argument.


View source
gapminder |> filter(year == 2007)
# is the same as:
filter(gapminder, year == 2007)



This means piping works for most tidyverse functions – and many others – but not universally. You will encounter base R functions that expect a vector, not a data frame, as their first argument.




This works:


View source
gapminder |>
  pull(lifeExp) |> # extracts a vector
  mean()


[1] 59.47444



This does not:


View source
# mean() expects a vector, not a tibble
gapminder |> mean() # Error


[1] NA





pull() extracts a single column as a vector – useful when you need to pipe into a base R function.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why summarise() Exists
Last week you used mean(), median(), and similar functions inside summarise(). Here is why that pattern is necessary.



Base R functions expect vectors:
# Works on a vector
mean(gapminder$lifeExp)

# Does not work on a tibble
gapminder |> mean()  # Error

summarise() bridges tibbles and vector functions:
# Correct: summarise feeds each column
# as a vector to the function inside
gapminder |>
  summarise(
    mean_le = mean(lifeExp),
    sd_le   = sd(lifeExp)
  )



summarise() takes a tibble in, applies functions column-by-column, and returns a tibble. It is the bridge between the tidyverse world and base R summary statistics.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Controlling How Much Prints
Tibbles print only 10 rows by default. You can override this with print():


# Print first 25 rows
gapminder |> print(n = 25)

# Print all rows
gapminder |> print(n = Inf)

# Print specific columns wide
gapminder |> print(width = Inf)

This is purely for interactive inspection – do not put print(n = Inf) in a document you are rendering. Rendered output should show only what your reader needs to see, controlled with head() or careful selection.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Wide vs. Long – A Reminder
Recall from last week: tidy data means one variable per column, one observation per row.
Wide format (common in spreadsheets, Excel exports, Eurostat downloads):




country
ghg_2020
ghg_2021
ghg_2022




Germany
9.0
9.4
9.3


France
5.6
5.9
5.7





Long (tidy) format (what the tidyverse expects):

year
ghg




Germany
2020
9.0


Germany
2021
9.4


France
2020
5.6


France
2021
5.9







In the wide version, year is hiding inside column names. ggplot2 and dplyr cannot see it there. You must reshape first.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
pivot_longer() – Wide to Long




View source
library(tidyverse)

ghg_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -country, # all columns except country
    names_to = "year", # new column for old headers
    values_to = "ghg" # new column for values
  )


Key arguments:

cols – which columns to reshape (everything that is not an ID variable)
names_to – what to call the new column that holds the old column names
values_to – what to call the new column that holds the values

title: QMIR 2026
text:
pivot_wider() – Long to Wide
The reverse: sometimes you need wide format for a table or for a specific model.




View source
ghg_long |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = "year", # column whose values become headers
    values_from = "ghg" # column whose values fill the cells
  )



Before:




country
year
ghg




Germany

Note


pivot_longer() is far more common in practice. Most raw data arrives wide – tidy analysis requires long.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Practical Example: What Happens When You Load Eurostat Data

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 1: Skip the Metadata Rows
We know from inspecting the file that the real column headers (TIME, 2019, …) are on row 10. So we skip 9 rows:




View source
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 9 # rows 1–9 are metadata
)

ghg_raw |> print(n = 5)


# A tibble: 38 × 5
  TIME                                      `2019` `2020` `2021` `2022`
  <chr>                                     <chr>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020) 7.8       7      7.4    7.3
2 Belgium                                   10.6      9.6    9.9    9.3
3 Bulgaria                                  6.8       5.9    6.9    7.7
4 Czechia                                   12.4     11.6   12     11.4
5 Denmark                                   8.6       7.7    7.7    7.4
# ℹ 33 more rows



Better – but notice:

The first column is named TIME (we want country)
Column names 2019, 2020, … start with a number
There are stray footer rows at the bottom (Special value, :)







Tip


How to find the right skip value: Open the file in Excel and count how many rows appear before the header row. Subtract 1 – that is your skip value.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 2: janitor::clean_names() to the Rescue
The janitor package’s clean_names() function converts all column names to safe, consistent snake_case automatically:




View source
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  skip = 9
) |>
  clean_names()

names(ghg_raw)


[1] "time"  "x2019" "x2020" "x2021" "x2022"




clean_names() converts 2019 → x2019, 2020 → x2020, etc. – adding an x prefix to columns that would otherwise start with a number.
This removes the backtick problem entirely. x2019 is a legal R column name that needs no special quoting.







Note


clean_names() also handles spaces, dashes, parentheses, and accented characters. Make it a habit to call it immediately after every read_excel() or read_csv().

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Step 3: Rename, Filter, Drop, Pivot


View source
ghg_clean <- ghg_raw |>
  rename(country = time) |> # TIME → country
  filter(!is.na(country), !country %in% c("Special value", ":")) |> # remove footer rows
  select(-x2019) |> # drop 2019 (not in GDP data)
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -country,
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "ghg_pc"
  ) |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(str_remove(year, "^x"))) # "x2020" → 2020

ghg_clean |> head(4)


# A tibble: 4 × 3
  country                                    year ghg_pc
  <chr>                                     <int>  <dbl>
1 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)  2020    7  
2 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)  2021    7.4
3 European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)  2022    7.3
4 Belgium                                    2020    9.6









Tip


str_remove(year, "^x") strips the leading x that clean_names() added, then as.integer() converts the result to a number. The ^ means “start of string” – a small taste of regular expressions, which we will return to later.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Alternative: janitor::row_to_names()
Sometimes you cannot easily count the number of skip rows, or the header row is somewhere in the middle of the sheet. janitor::row_to_names() handles this differently: load everything, then promote a specific row to become the column names.




View source
# Load without skipping anything
ghg_raw <- read_excel(
  "data/Eurostat_GHGpc.xlsx",
  sheet = "Sheet 1",
  col_names = FALSE # don't treat row 1 as headers
)

# Promote row 10 to column names, drop rows above it
ghg_raw <- ghg_raw |>
  row_to_names(row_number = 10, remove_rows_above = TRUE) |>
  clean_names()




row_to_names() arguments:

row_number – which row contains the real headers
remove_rows_above = TRUE – drops all metadata rows above it automatically








Note


Both approaches (skip = and row_to_names()) achieve the same result. Use whichever is clearer for the file you are working with. skip = is simpler when the header row is fixed and predictable. row_to_names() is more robust when the file structure is irregular or when you are not sure how many rows to skip.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why This Matters for Pivoting





country
x2020
x2021
x2022




Germany
9.0
9.4
9.3


France
5.6
5.9
5.7



Without clean_names(), the year columns would be named 2020, 2021 – requiring backticks everywhere. With clean_names(), they become x2020, x2021, which R handles without complaint.

After (long / tidy):



country
year
ghg_pc




Germany
2020
9.0


Germany
2021
9.4


Germany
2022
9.3


France
2020
5.6



Once pivoted, year is just a regular variable column. No backticks, no special treatment needed.






Note


The backtick trap still exists in R – column names starting with numbers or containing spaces must be wrapped in backticks. clean_names() is the easiest way to avoid it entirely.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Why Join?
Real research rarely lives in a single dataset. You might have:

GHG emissions from Eurostat in one file
GDP per capita from Eurostat in another file
EU accession dates from Wikipedia
Country codes from a reference package


Joining combines these into a single analysis-ready dataset by matching rows on a shared key – usually a country name, country code, or year.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Four Main Joins
All joins take two tables and a key (the column(s) to match on).







Join
What it keeps




left_join(x, y)
All rows from x; matching rows from y; NA where no match


right_join(x, y)
All rows from y; matching rows from x; NA where no match


inner_join(x, y)
Only rows that match in both tables


full_join(x, y)
All rows from both; NA wherever there is no match










Tip


In practice, left_join() is by far the most common. Start with your main analysis dataset on the left, and add variables from auxiliary datasets on the right. Rows in your main dataset are always preserved.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
left_join() – An Example




View source
# Both datasets are now tidy long format
# with matching columns: country + year

eu_data <- ghg |>
  left_join(gdp, by = join_by(country, year))


The by argument names the key columns – the columns that must match for two rows to be joined.
If the key column has different names in the two tables:
left_join(x, y, by = join_by(country == nation))

After joining, always verify the result:
# Check dimensions make sense
nrow(eu_data)
glimpse(eu_data)

# Check for unexpected NAs introduced by the join
eu_data |>
  filter(is.na(gdp_pc)) |>
  distinct(country, year)






Important


Always compare row counts before and after joining.
nrow(ghg)      # rows before
nrow(eu_data)  # rows after left_join
In a left_join, the row count of the result must equal the row count of the left table. If it is larger, your key has duplicates on the right side. If it is smaller, something went wrong. Check immediately – silent row loss is one of the most common data errors.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Visualising the Joins


x (GHG data):


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  country   ghg
  <chr>   <dbl>
1 Germany   9.3
2 France    5.7
3 Poland    9.4


y (GDP data):


# A tibble: 3 × 2
  country   gdp
  <chr>   <dbl>
1 Germany   117
2 France    103
3 Spain      86



left_join(x, y) – keeps all of x:


# A tibble: 3 × 3
  country   ghg   gdp
  <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>
1 Germany   9.3   117
2 France    5.7   103
3 Poland    9.4    NA


inner_join(x, y) – only matches:


# A tibble: 2 × 3
  country   ghg   gdp
  <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>
1 Germany   9.3   117
2 France    5.7   103

title: QMIR 2026
text:
NA in R
In R, missing values are represented as NA – not available.


View source
x <- c(1, 2, NA, 4)

is.na(x) # TRUE for each NA position
sum(is.na(x)) # count of NAs
mean(x) # NA -- most functions propagate NA by default
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) # 2.33 -- explicitly remove NAs




NA is not zero. It means the value is unknown.
NA is contagious. Any arithmetic involving NA returns NA.
na.rm = TRUE is the standard argument to ignore NAs in summary functions.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Finding and Filtering Missing Values




View source
# Rows where a variable IS missing
data |>
  filter(is.na(gdp_pc))

# Rows where a variable is NOT missing
data |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp_pc))

# Count NAs per column
data |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.))))





View source
# Remove rows with any NA
data |> drop_na()

# Remove rows with NA in specific column
data |> drop_na(gdp_pc)








Warning


drop_na() silently removes data. Before using it, check how many rows it would remove and ask whether those missing values are informative. A country that never reported GHG emissions is not the same as a country with a data entry error.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Problem with Imputation
A common temptation: replace NA with the column mean.


View source
data |>
  mutate(gdp_pc = ifelse(is.na(gdp_pc), mean(gdp_pc, na.rm = TRUE), gdp_pc))



What assumption does this imply?

You are asserting that the missing country’s GDP is average – but you have no evidence of that.
Mean imputation compresses variance and distorts correlations.
It treats missingness as random, which is rarely true. Poor countries may be less likely to report data.









Important


There is no free lunch with missing data. Every approach – dropping, imputing, or modelling – makes an assumption. The correct response is to be transparent: report how many observations are missing and why you handled them the way you did. Never silently drop or impute without acknowledgment.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Coding Missings on Import
Many datasets use special codes for missing values: -99, 999, "n/a", "." (Stata).


View source
# Tell readr which strings to treat as NA
my_data <- read_csv(
  "data/file.csv",
  na = c("", "NA", "-99", "n/a", ".")
)

# For Stata files, haven handles this automatically
library(haven)
my_data <- read_dta("data/file.dta")

# After loading, check for suspicious values
summary(my_data)









Tip


Always open your raw data file before loading it in R. A two-minute inspection in Excel or a text editor tells you the structure, encoding, missing value codes, and any metadata rows – exactly the information you need to load it correctly.

title: QMIR 2026
QMIR 2026
text:
Cleaning Country Names & Factors


View source
eu_data <- eu_data |>
  mutate(
    country = fct(country),
    country = fct_recode(
      country,
      "EU27" = "European Union - 27 countries (from 2020)"
    )
  )




# A tibble: 35 × 2
  country      n
  <fct>    <int>
1 EU27         3
2 Belgium      3
3 Bulgaria     3
4 Czechia      3
5 Denmark      3
# ℹ 30 more rows



Why factors? Factors store a fixed set of categories. They are essential for correct plot ordering, legend display, and later for regression dummy coding. fct_recode() renames specific levels without touching the others.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Recap: The Three Layers
Every ggplot is assembled from the same building blocks:
ggplot(data, aes(x = ..., y = ..., color = ...)) +
  geom_*() +
  scale_*() +
  labs() +
  theme_*()









Layer
Purpose




ggplot() + aes()
Canvas: data and variable-to-visual mappings


geom_*()
What to draw: points, lines, bars, boxes, text, …


scale_*()
How to map: axis limits, colors, log transforms, …


labs()
Text: title, subtitle, axis labels, captions, legend title


theme_*()
Appearance: fonts, gridlines, background, …

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Main Aesthetics
aes() maps variables to visual properties. The main ones:




Aesthetic
What it controls




x, y
Position on axes


color
Outline / line / point color


fill
Interior fill color (bars, boxes, ribbons)


alpha
Transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = opaque)


size
Point size or line thickness


shape
Point shape (circle, triangle, square, …)


linetype
Line style (solid, dashed, dotted, …)


label
Text label (used with geom_text())


group
Grouping without visual distinction











Note


Inside aes() = mapped to data. Outside aes() = fixed constant.
geom_point(color = "red") makes all points red.
geom_point(aes(color = continent)) colors by the continent variable.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Key Geoms






Geom
Use case




geom_point()
Scatterplot


geom_line()
Time series, trends


geom_col()
Bar chart (uses your values)


geom_bar()
Bar chart (counts rows)


geom_boxplot()
Distribution summary


geom_histogram()
Distribution of one variable


geom_density()
Smoothed distribution


geom_smooth()
Trend / regression line


geom_text()
Text labels at data positions


geom_hline() / geom_vline()
Reference lines





Geoms can be layered:
ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
And combined with group aesthetics:
ggplot(data, aes(x = year,
                 y = value,
                 color = country)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Scales
Scales control how data values translate into visual properties.


Axis scales:
scale_x_log10()        # log transform x axis
scale_y_continuous(
  limits = c(0, 20),   # axis range
  breaks = seq(0, 20, 5) # tick marks
)
scale_x_discrete()     # for factor axes
Color scales:
scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1")
scale_color_manual(values = c("red", "blue"))
scale_fill_viridis_d()  # colorblind-friendly


Why scales matter:

A log scale compresses a skewed distribution, revealing patterns hidden on a linear scale
Color choice affects readability and accessibility – colorblind viewers cannot distinguish red and green
viridis palettes are colorblind-safe and print well in grayscale








Tip


For color-coded plots, always ask: would this still be readable printed in black and white, or by someone with red-green color blindness?

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Labels and Titles
labs(
  title    = "GHG Emissions and GDP per Capita, EU Member States",
  subtitle = "Data: Eurostat, 2020–2022",
  x        = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
  y        = "GHG Emissions per Capita (tonnes)",
  color    = "Membership",
  caption  = "Note: EU27 average excluded."
)








Element
Guideline




Title
Describe what the plot shows, not what you conclude


Subtitle
Source, year, or key qualification


Axis labels
Always include units. “GDP” is ambiguous; “GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)” is not


Caption
Data source, methodological notes, exclusions


Legend title
Rename via color =, fill = etc. in labs()

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Themes
Themes control the non-data appearance of the plot.


Built-in themes:
theme_bw()       # white background, grey gridlines
theme_minimal()  # minimal gridlines, no border
theme_classic()  # white background, no gridlines
theme_void()     # blank canvas (for maps, diagrams)

Customising a theme:
theme_bw() +
theme(
  plot.title      = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
  axis.title      = element_text(size = 11),
  legend.position = "bottom",
  panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),  # remove minor gridlines
  aspect.ratio    = 0.618              # golden ratio height/width
)








Tip


For academic work, theme_bw() or theme_minimal() are good defaults. Avoid dark backgrounds for documents that will be printed. Set legend.position = "bottom" for plots with many categories – it preserves horizontal space.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Aspect Ratio – Does It Matter?
Which of these two plots do you prefer? (They show identical data.)








Note


Plot B uses aspect.ratio = 0.618 – the golden ratio (~1:1.618 width to height). Many designers and scientists find this proportion visually pleasing and it is a good default for scatter plots. For time series, a wider aspect ratio often reads better. There is no universal rule – but being deliberate about it matters.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Example: Scatterplot with Regression Line




View source
eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, country != "EU27") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_pc, y = ghg_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "steelblue") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
    y = "GHG Emissions per Capita (tonnes)",
    title = "Wealth and Emissions in the EU, 2022",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  ) +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 0.618)








Figure 1: GDP and GHG emissions, EU member states 2022







Key elements:

geom_smooth(method = "lm") adds a linear regression line with a confidence band (se = TRUE)
alpha = 0.7 reduces overplotting
size = 2 makes points more visible
country != "EU27" excludes the aggregate from the point cloud

The positive slope suggests richer EU member states tend to emit more GHG per capita – though with substantial variation.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Example: Bar Chart with Custom Fill
text:
Example: Bar Chart with Custom Fill




View source
eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, !is.na(ghg_pc), ghg_pc > 0) |>
  mutate(
    is_eu27 = country == "EU27",
    country = fct_reorder(country, ghg_pc)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = country, y = ghg_pc, fill = is_eu27)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "steelblue", "TRUE" = "firebrick"),
    labels = c("Member State", "EU27 Avg"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "GHG per Capita (tonnes)",
    title = "GHG Emissions by EU Member State, 2022",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  )








Figure 2: GHG emissions per capita by EU member state, 2022






Key elements:

fct_reorder(country, ghg_pc) sorts countries by emission value
fill = is_eu27 maps a logical variable to color
scale_fill_manual() assigns specific colors and renames the legend
coord_flip() rotates axes – equivalent to swapping x and y in aes(), but keeps your code readable when you are building a horizontal bar chart from a vertical one

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Facets
facet_wrap() splits a plot into small multiples by a grouping variable.




View source
eu_data |>
  filter(country != "EU27") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_pc, y = ghg_pc)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~year) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (EU27 = 100)",
    y = "GHG per Capita (tonnes)",
    caption = "Source: Eurostat"
  )








Figure 3: GDP vs. GHG by year, EU member states






facet_wrap(~ year) creates one panel per year.
facet_grid(row ~ col) for a two-dimensional grid.
Free vs. fixed scales:
# Each panel uses the same axes (default)
facet_wrap(~ year)

# Each panel scales to its own data
facet_wrap(~ year, scales = "free")






Warning


Free scales make within-panel patterns clearer, but make cross-panel comparison impossible. Be explicit in your figure caption about which you are using and why.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The .gitignore File
A .gitignore file tells Git which files to never track. Place it in the root of your repository.
# .gitignore

# R temporary files
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/

# Rendered outputs (optional -- only if you regenerate from source)
*.pdf
*.html

# Large data files (if you store data elsewhere)
data/*.csv
data/*.xlsx

# OS files
.DS_Store        # Mac
Thumbs.db        # Windows







Tip


Never commit sensitive data (passwords, API keys, personal data) to a Git repository. Even private repositories can be accidentally made public. Add such files to .gitignore immediately.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Analytical Problem-Solving
When you face a data analysis task, divide and conquer.

Example: How do EU member states’ GHG emissions relate to their wealth?


Break this into sub-problems:


How do I load the GHG and GDP data into R? (import)
The data is wide – how do I reshape it? (pivot)
The country names differ slightly – how do I merge? (join)
I need a new column for membership status (mutate)
How do I visualise the relationship? (ggplot)
Each of these can be broken down further…










Tip


After each sub-problem:

Test in the console – does the intermediate result look right?
Render your document – does it still compile?
Commit to Git – you have a working checkpoint to fall back to

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A Practical Workflow Loop
1. Set up .qmd → render once → confirm it works
2. Write a small chunk → run in console → check result
3. When chunk is correct → render full document
4. Commit to Git with a clear message: "add data import step"
5. Repeat

Why render frequently? Problems compound. A chunk that works in isolation may fail in a rendered document because of:

Missing library() calls
Objects that exist in your environment but are not created in the document
Chunk execution order dependencies









Tip


Rule of thumb: If you would be upset to lose the last 20 minutes of work, commit it. Commits are free.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Inline Code in Quarto
You can embed R results directly in prose using the `{r} expression` syntax. Quarto evaluates the expression and inserts the result as text:

The dataset contains {r} nrow(eu_data) observations across {r} n_distinct(eu_data$country) countries.
The mean GHG per capita in 2022 was {r} eu_data |> filter(year == 2022) |> pull(ghg_pc) |> mean(na.rm = TRUE) |> round(1) tonnes.


The above renders as:

The dataset contains 105 observations across 35 countries. The mean GHG per capita in 2022 was 7.6 tonnes.









Tip


Inline code means your text and your numbers never get out of sync. When the data changes, the prose updates automatically on the next render. Use this for all specific numbers you report – never type a number by hand that R could compute for you.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
janitor: Beyond clean_names()


tabyl() – a tidy frequency table:


View source
eu_data |>
  filter(!is.na(ghg_pc)) |>
  tabyl(year)


 year  n   percent
 2020 28 0.3333333
 2021 28 0.3333333
 2022 28 0.3333333



adorn_totals() adds a totals row (or column):


View source
eu_data |>
  filter(!is.na(ghg_pc)) |>
  tabyl(year) |>
  adorn_totals("row")


  year  n   percent
  2020 28 0.3333333
  2021 28 0.3333333
  2022 28 0.3333333
 Total 84 1.0000000

title: QMIR 2026
text:
tabyl → adorn_totals() → kable()
One pipeline, publication-ready output:



View source
eu_data |>
  filter(!is.na(ghg_pc), country != "EU27") |>
  mutate(high_emitter = ghg_pc > median(ghg_pc, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  tabyl(year, high_emitter) |>
  adorn_totals(c("row", "col")) |>
  kable(
    col.names = c("Year", "Below Median", "Above Median", "Total"),
    caption = "EU member states by emission level relative to median"
  )



EU member states by emission level relative to median


Year
Below Median
Above Median
Total




2020
15
12
27


2021
14
13
27


2022
14
13
27


Total
43
38
81













Tip


tabyl() → adorn_totals() → kable() is one of the fastest ways to produce a clean, publication-ready summary table. adorn_totals(c("row", "col")) adds totals to both margins at once.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
kable() for Printed Tables
For tables in rendered documents, knitr::kable() produces clean output in both HTML and PDF:





View source
eu_data |>
  filter(year == 2022, country != "EU27") |>
  slice_max(ghg_pc, n = 5) |>
  select(country, ghg_pc, gdp_pc) |>
  kable(
    col.names = c("Country", "GHG pc (tonnes)", "GDP pc (EU27=100)"),
    digits = 1,
    caption = "Top 5 emitters in 2022"
  )



Top 5 emitters in 2022


Country
GHG pc (tonnes)
GDP pc (EU27=100)




Luxembourg
14.5
256


Ireland
13.0
235


Czechia
11.4
90


Estonia
10.7
85


Cyprus
10.2
94








kable() arguments:

col.names – rename columns in the output
digits – round numbers
caption – table caption (cross-referenceable with tbl- label)
format = "latex" / "html" – rarely needed, Quarto detects format automatically

For more control: the kableExtra package adds column alignment, row grouping, and color highlighting.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
stringr Quick Reference
stringr is the tidyverse package for working with text. All functions start with str_.


Detect & filter:
str_detect(x, "pattern")   # TRUE/FALSE -- use in filter()
str_starts(x, "A")         # starts with "A"
str_ends(x, "ia")          # ends with "ia"
str_count(x, "pattern")    # count occurrences
Extract & split:
str_sub(x, 1, 3)            # characters 1–3
str_extract(x, "[0-9]+")    # first regex match
str_split(x, "_")           # split on delimiter

Replace & clean:
str_replace(x, "old", "new")   # replace first match
str_replace_all(x, "old","new")# replace all matches
str_remove(x, "^x")            # remove first match
str_remove_all(x, " ")         # remove all spaces
str_to_lower(x)                # lowercase
str_to_upper(x)                # uppercase
str_to_title(x)                # Title Case
str_trim(x)                    # remove leading/trailing spaces
str_pad(x, width = 5,          # pad to fixed width
        side = "left", pad="0")








Note


You already used str_remove(year, "^x") in this session. The ^ is a regular expression anchor meaning “start of string”. Regular expressions are a powerful pattern language – we will return to them later, but str_detect() and str_remove() will cover most of your immediate needs.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
dplyr Select Helpers
select() accepts helper functions that match columns by name pattern – far more powerful than listing columns one by one.


# Columns that start with a prefix
data |> select(starts_with("ghg"))

# Columns that end with a suffix
data |> select(ends_with("_pc"))

# Columns containing a substring
data |> select(contains("year"))

# Columns matching a regex
data |> select(matches("^x[0-9]{4}$"))

# A range of consecutive columns
data |> select(country:gdp_pc)

# Everything except named columns
data |> select(-country, -year)

# Reorder: put country first, keep rest
data |> select(country, everything())

These helpers also work inside mutate(across(...)) and summarise(across(...)):
# Round all numeric columns ending in _pc
data |>
  mutate(across(ends_with("_pc"), round, 1))

# Mean of all columns starting with ghg
data |>
  summarise(across(starts_with("ghg"), mean,
                   na.rm = TRUE))






Tip


where() is another powerful helper – it selects columns by type:
data |> select(where(is.numeric))
data |> select(where(is.character))

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Conditional Variables: if_else() and case_when()

Two tidyverse tools for creating variables whose value depends on a condition.


if_else() — one condition, two outcomes:
gap_2007 |>
  mutate(
    rich = if_else(gdpPercap > 20000,
                   "High income",
                   "Low/mid income")
  )

First argument: the condition
Second: value if TRUE
Third: value if FALSE







Note


Prefer if_else() over base R ifelse() in tidyverse pipelines — it is stricter about types and handles NA more predictably.




case_when() — multiple conditions:
gap_2007 |>
  mutate(
    income_group = case_when(
      gdpPercap > 30000 ~ "High",
      gdpPercap > 10000 ~ "Middle",
      gdpPercap >  3000 ~ "Low",
      .default          =  "Very low"
    )
  )

Conditions are evaluated top to bottom
First TRUE condition wins — order matters
.default catches everything not matched above








Tip


Both functions work inside mutate() and return a new column. Use if_else() for simple yes/no splits. Switch to case_when() as soon as you have three or more outcomes — a chain of nested if_else() calls becomes unreadable fast.

text:
QMIR 2026

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Week 3 Goals
By the end of today, you should:

Understand the core concepts: objects, functions, and pipes
Know the main data types R uses to store information
Know the main data structures R uses to organize information
Be able to access and explore data using [] and $

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Agenda

Leftovers from Last Week
Objects, Functions & Pipes
Data Types
Data Structures
Accessing & Exploring Data
Walkthrough & Exercises

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Leftovers from Last Week

Cloning from GitHub

This is how you access and submit the homeworks and take home exam

Creating and linking your private projects with GitHub
Habits for effective and efficient coding:

learn to use shortcuts (e.g. opening and closing Positron panels)
learn to use your keyboard (e.g. highlighting text, adding code cells)
set theme and font that makes you feel good



Budget ~10 minutes

title: QMIR 2026
section: R as a Language for Storing and Transforming Information
text:
R as a Language for Storing and Transforming Information

To understand […] R, two slogans are helpful:
Everything that exists is an object
Everything that happens is a function call
– John Chambers


You give R objects – named containers holding information
You apply functions – operations that transform objects
You chain steps together using pipes


This quote is a good anchor. The point is that R has a very consistent grammar once you understand these three ideas.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Objects – Storing Information


An object has:

a name (how you refer to it)
a value (the information stored)
a type (what kind of information)


An R example:


View source
# Assign a value to a name with <-
country <- "Germany"

gdp_per_capita <- 54300

is_eu_member <- TRUE




The arrow <- is read as “gets”. country gets the value “Germany”. Emphasize that nothing is printed when you assign — you have to call the name to see it.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Objects – Storing Information


View source
# print by typing the name
country


[1] "Germany"


View source
gdp_per_capita


[1] 54300


View source
is_eu_member


[1] TRUE

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Functions – Doing Things with Objects


A function:

takes one or more inputs (arguments)
does something
returns an output

function_name(argument1, argument2, ...)




View source
gdp_values <- c(54300, 43800, 33200)

# Functions transform objects
mean(gdp_values)


[1] 43766.67


View source
sqrt(gdp_per_capita)


[1] 233.0236


View source
nchar(country)


[1] 7

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Functions – Doing Things with Objects


You can write your own functions.
Functions can be anything from simple one-liners to complex software components. Among other things, they can be:

small personal helpers

reusable analysis tools
shared with others

Collections of useful, published functions form packages. We will expore some next week.
Packages are what make R – like Python and many other programming languages – a powerful ecosystem.




View source
# A small custom helper
add_tax <- function(price) {
  price * 1.19
}

add_tax(10)


[1] 11.9

title: QMIR 2026
text: Recap

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Getting help
To open the documentation page of a function,
help(function)
?function()
for example:


View source
help(mean)
?mean()

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Control Structures in R








Structure
Syntax Example
Description




if
if (x > 0) { ... }
Executes code if condition is TRUE


if-else
if (x > 0) { ... } else { ... }
Chooses between two branches


ifelse
ifelse(x > 0, "pos", "neg")
Vectorized conditional


for
for (i in 1:5) { print(i) }
Loop over a sequence


while
while (x < 10) { x <- x + 1 }
Repeat while condition TRUE


repeat
repeat { ...; if (x>10) break }
Infinite loop until break


break
break
Exit a loop immediately


next
next
Skip current iteration in a loop

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Pipe – Chaining Operations
Without the pipe, nested calls become hard to read:


View source
round(mean(sqrt(gdp_values)), digits = 2)


[1] 208.17


With the pipe |>, read left to right – “and then”:


View source
gdp_values |> sqrt() |> mean() |> round(digits = 2)


[1] 208.17




The pipe says: take this, and then do that.


The native pipe |> was introduced in R 4.1. Students may also encounter %>% from the magrittr package — functionally very similar. For this course, |> is preferred.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Comments – Code as Communication


View source
# BAD: comments that just repeat the code
x <- x + 1 # add 1 to x

# GOOD: comments that explain WHY
x <- x + 1 # index starts at 0, shift to 1-based for plotting

# Use comments to:
# - explain decisions ("why log-transform? right skew in raw data")
# - mark sections of your script
# - temporarily disable code during testing




Your most important collaborator is your future self — six months from now, you will not remember why you did what you did. Thanks to our Quarto literate programming workflow, you are living in the golden age of commenting – you can literally write full prose between code cells instead of apologizing in hashtags later.

title: QMIR 2026
section: What Kind of Information Does R Store?
text:
What Kind of Information Does R Store?



Type
Description
Example




double
Decimal numbers
3.14, 54300.5


integer
Whole numbers
42, 2026


character
Text / strings
"Germany", "hello"


logical
True or false
TRUE, FALSE


factor
Categories
"low", "medium", "high"


Date
Calendar dates
2026-02-23

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Numeric: Double & Integer


View source
gdp <- 54300.5 # double (default for numbers)
seats <- 736L # integer (the L suffix makes it explicit)

class(gdp)


[1] "numeric"


View source
class(seats)


[1] "integer"




For most purposes in this course, the integer/double distinction doesn’t matter – R handles it behind the scenes. But it’s good to know it exists.
It is important that you know how to find out if objects are numeric.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Character / String


View source
capital <- 'Berlin' # single or double quotes both work
country <- "Germany" # but double quotes are sometimes easier to see

nchar(country) # how many characters?


[1] 7


View source
toupper(country) # string manipulation


[1] "GERMANY"


View source
paste(country, "is in the EU")


[1] "Germany is in the EU"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Character / String
Numbers stored as characters are not numbers – R won’t do math with them:


View source
"42" + 1 # this does not work


Error in `"42" + 1`:
! non-numeric argument to binary operator


View source
as.numeric("42") + 1 # transforming the string to numeric makes it work


[1] 43

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Logical / Boolean


View source
is_eu_member <- TRUE

is_eu_member


[1] TRUE


View source
population <- 84000000

# Comparisons produce logical values
population > 50000000


[1] TRUE


View source
population == 84000000


[1] TRUE


View source
!is_eu_member # NOT


[1] FALSE


Logicals are the backbone of filtering – you’ll use them constantly when subsetting data.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Logical Operators








Operator
Meaning
Example




<
Less than
3 < 5


<=
Less than or equal
3 <= 3


>
Greater than
5 > 3


>=
Greater than or equal
5 >= 5


==
Equal to
3 == 3


!=
Not equal to
3 != 5


&
AND (vectorised)
(3 > 1) & (5 > 2)


\|
OR (vectorised)
(3 > 5) \| (5 > 2)


!
NOT
!(3 > 5)


%in%
Is element contained in vector
"Germany" %in% c("France", "Germany")

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Specialized Types: Factors & Dates


Factors – categorical variables with fixed levels


View source
regime <- factor(
  c("democracy", "autocracy", "democracy", "hybrid"),
  levels = c("autocracy", "hybrid", "democracy")
)

regime


[1] democracy autocracy democracy hybrid   
Levels: autocracy hybrid democracy


View source
levels(regime)


[1] "autocracy" "hybrid"    "democracy"



Dates


View source
today <- Sys.Date()
today


[1] "2026-02-22"


View source
treaty <- as.Date("1957-03-25") # Treaty of Rome
treaty


[1] "1957-03-25"


View source
today - treaty # date arithmetic!


Time difference of 25171 days




You’ll encounter these often in real data. The key insight: R treats them differently from plain text so it can do useful things like ordering, arithmetic, and plotting.


Don’t go too deep here. Factors will come up naturally in visualization (Week 4). lubridate for dates can be mentioned as an optional depth topic.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
How Is Information Organized?










Structure
Dimensions
Types allowed
Formal notation
Other names (across fields)




Vector
1
One
\(\mathbf{x}_i\)
feature vector, embedding, signal, series


Matrix
2
One

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Vectors – The Building Block


View source
# c() combines values into a vector
countries <- c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Turkey")
population <- c(84, 68, 60, 47, 88) # millions
eu_member <- c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)

length(countries) # number of elements in vector


[1] 5


View source
class(countries) # check type


[1] "character"


View source
class(eu_member) # check type


[1] "logical"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Vectors – The Building Block
A vector can only hold one type. If you mix types, R silently converts:


View source
mixed <- c(1, 2, "three") # all become character!
mixed


[1] "1"     "2"     "three"

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Matrices & Arrays – Briefly


View source
matrix(1:6, nrow = 2, ncol = 3)


     [,1] [,2] [,3]
[1,]    1    3    5
[2,]    2    4    6


View source
matrix(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), nrow = 2, ncol = 2)


     [,1] [,2]
[1,] "A"  "C" 
[2,] "B"  "D" 



2D structures storing elements of the same type

Fundamental for linear algebra and numerical computations

Data frames are more flexible for tabular data, but matrices remain crucial in statistical modeling


Keep this brief — 2 minutes max. It’s here for conceptual completeness.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Frames – The Workhorse



Rectangular (rows × columns)
Each column is a vector (one type)
Columns can be different types from each other
Each row is an observation




View source
eu_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Turkey"),
  population = c(84, 68, 60, 47, 88),
  eu_member = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),
  regime = c("democracy", "democracy", "democracy", "democracy", "hybrid")
)

eu_data


  country population eu_member    regime
1 Germany         84      TRUE democracy
2  France         68      TRUE democracy
3   Italy         60      TRUE democracy
4   Spain         47      TRUE democracy
5  Turkey         88     FALSE    hybrid

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Lists – The Flexible Container


View source
country_info <- list(
  name = "Germany",
  population = 84000000,
  eu_member = TRUE,
  parties = c("SPD", "CDU", "Greens", "FDP")
)

country_info


$name
[1] "Germany"

$population
[1] 8.4e+07

$eu_member
[1] TRUE

$parties
[1] "SPD"    "CDU"    "Greens" "FDP"   


Lists can hold anything – including other lists. They’re less intuitive than data frames, but you’ll encounter them constantly as outputs of statistical models.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Reaching Inside Structures
Two main tools:

[ ] – index by position or condition
$ – index by name (for data frames and lists)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Indexing Vectors with [ ]




View source
countries <- c("Germany", "France", "Italy", "Spain", "Poland")

countries[1] # first element


[1] "Germany"


View source
countries[c(1, 3)] # first and third


[1] "Germany" "Italy"  


View source
countries[-2] # everything except second


[1] "Germany" "Italy"   "Spain"   "Poland" 





View source
population <- c(84, 68, 60, 47, 38)

# Logical indexing -- filter by condition
countries[population > 50]


[1] "Germany" "France"  "Italy"

text:
Indexing DFs with [ ] and $




View source
# [row, column] -- leave blank to mean "all"
eu_data[1, ] # first row, all columns

[1] 84

eu_data$country

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Walkthrough Overview

Clone the Week 3 exercise repo from GitHub (same workflow as last week)
Open exercise_week03.qmd in Positron
Work through the tasks in order
Fast finishers: optional depth exercises at the bottom of the document


Live-code the clone step briefly to warm students up. Then let them work. Circulate and help. Reserve ~10 min at the end for questions before previewing homework.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Exercise Structure
Core tasks (everyone):

Create vectors of different types and check their class
Build a small data frame from scratch – use a political science example
Subset with [ ] and $
Write clean, commented code throughout

Optional exercises:

Working with factors: level ordering and table()
Date arithmetic with as.Date()

Optional advanced exercises:

if-conditions and for-loops

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Homework

Distributed via GitHub (same workflow as Week 2)
Submit a rendered Quarto document (.qmd + rendered PDF)
Due: one week from today

The homework follows the same arc as today – you’ll work with a small political dataset to practice types, structures, and subsetting. Read the task descriptions carefully and write comments explaining your reasoning.

How do you usually spend your time outside university? (This can be anything: a side job, vocational/civilian service, volunteering, sports, creative hobbies, gaming, family time – whatever is part of your life)

title: QMIR 2026
text: About you continued

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What you will learn

How to build a reproducible, open-source research workflow – integrating data, code, analysis, and writing into a transparent scientific process
How to use statistical models to learn from data – understanding what models assume, what they estimate, how they update our beliefs, and how to interpret results responsibly
How to communicate empirical results clearly and responsibly – through well-structured reports, visualizations, and reproducible documents

title: QMIR 2026
section: What this course does not cover
text:
What this course does not cover

Data collection and research design – we work with existing datasets and do not cover survey design, measurement strategies, or fieldwork (the university offers dedicated courses and resources in these areas)
Causal inference methods – we focus on analyzing and interpreting associations between variables rather than formal causal identification (specialized courses cover causal inference in depth)
Substantive political theory or normative argumentation – we use real political science examples, but the focus is methodological and conceptual training rather than developing theoretical frameworks

text:
Why the course is structured this way

An (toy) example of a fully integrated, reproducible research pipeline in action: Quarto manuscript
We will build up to this step by step, learning and integrating each piece along the way

title: QMIR 2026
section: Why the course is structured this way
text:
Why the course is structured this way

Admittedly overkill for simple tasks, but it provides a scalable, FOSS toolbox for complex projects, advanced models, and fully reproducible workflows

Builds a foundation in quantitative methods and data science that’s useful across disciplines and careers, helping you understand research from other fields while gaining essential data skills

title: Getting Started: Installation Guide
text: This course introduces you to a range of software tools which, taken together, form a powerful and contemporary toolbox for transparent, reproducible, and scalable empirical research. Rather than relying on a single piece of software, you will learn how different tools interact and complement one another — much like they do in real-world research workflows.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Reproducible Research?



Reproducible research means:

Every results can be regenerated from raw data
Every transformation and coding decision is documented
Every table and figure can be recreated automatically
Analytical choices are transparent and traceable




Why this matters:

Reviewers can understand and verify your analysis
Coauthors can follow and extend your work
You can easily test alternative specifications (e.g., add variable X)
Your future self knows exactly what you did
If your laptop dies tomorrow, nothing is lost

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Version Control with Git

Repository (repo) \(\rightarrow\) project folder with memory 🗂️
git init \(\rightarrow\) Turns a normal folder into a Git repository 🚀
Stage (git add) \(\rightarrow\) Select changes you want to include in the next snapshot 📦
Commit \(\rightarrow\) A saved snapshot of staged changes 📸
Commit message \(\rightarrow\) Short explanation of what and why you changed something 💬
History (git log) \(\rightarrow\) Timeline of all committed snapshots 🕰️
Remote \(\rightarrow\) Online copy of your repository (e.g., GitHub) ☁️

analysis_final.R
analysis_final2.R
analysis_REALLY_final.R

draft1.pdf
draft2.pdf
draft2_final.pdf
This is manual version control – and it breaks down quickly.

Remember: Git tracks changes inside files – not by duplicating them

Git & GitHub – A Folder Structure Reminder
A few students ran into problems with last week’s homework. Here is a summary of how to set things up correctly.
One dedicated QMIR folder, at a stable path on your computer:
Documents/
  QMIR_2026/  ← your top-level course folder. Individual repos go INSIDE here, e.g:
    hw-w04/ 
    lab-w04/
    ...

✅ Place individual repositories inside the QMIR folder
❌ Do not turn the top-level QMIR_2026/ folder itself into a Git repo — Git repos inside Git repos cause problems
❌ Do not place your QMIR folder inside a cloud-synced folder (iCloud Drive, Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox) — syncing services and Git conflict with each other and corrupt repositories

The Tidyverse – A Coherent Ecosystem


Last week: packages are collections of functions.
The tidyverse is a collection of packages that share:

a common philosophy of how data should be structured
a consistent grammar (functions behave predictably)
seamless interoperability – they are designed to work together



View source
library(tidyverse)


One library() call loads the core set.




Source: Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel (2020)

section: The Tibble – A Better Data Frame
text:
The Tibble – A Better Data Frame


The tidyverse uses tibbles instead of base R data frames.
They are the same idea – rows × columns, each column one type – but with better defaults:

Prints only the first 10 rows
Shows column types below the header
Never converts strings to factors silently
Never creates row names

title: QMIR 2026
text:
What is Tidy Data?

Tidy datasets are all alike, but every messy dataset is messy in its own way.
– Hadley Wickham


A dataset is tidy when:

Each variable has its own column
Each observation has its own row
Each value has its own cell

title: QMIR 2026
section: Tidy vs. Wide Data – An Example
text:
Tidy vs. Wide Data – An Example
Wide format (common in spreadsheets, not tidy):



country
gdpPercap_1952
gdpPercap_1957
gdpPercap_1962




Germany
7144
10188
12902


France
7030
8663
10560




Long (tidy) format (what the tidyverse expects):



country
year
gdpPercap




Germany
1952
7144


Germany
1957
10188


France
1952
7030


France
1957
8663






In the tidy version, year is a variable – a column. In the wide version, year is hiding inside column names. The tidyverse (and especially ggplot2) expects tidy data.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Reshaping with tidyr – A Brief Preview
text:
Reshaping with tidyr – A Brief Preview
You will encounter wide data in the wild. tidyr provides two functions to convert between formats:

pivot_longer() – wide → long (tidy)
pivot_wider() – long → wide



View source
# Hypothetical wide dataset
wide_data |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("gdpPercap_"),
    names_to = "year",
    values_to = "gdpPercap"
  )



We won’t go deep on reshaping today – the gapminder data is already tidy. But knowing these functions exist will save you hours when you start working with real-world data.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Data Science Cycle


Everything we do in this course fits somewhere in this cycle:
Import → Tidy → Transform → Visualise → Model → Communicate
Where we are today:

✅ Import – readr
✅ Tidy – we got lucky, gapminder is already tidy
✅ Transform – dplyr
👀 Visualise – ggplot2 preview
📅 Model – coming later in the course
✅ Communicate – Quarto, all along





Source: Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Grolemund (2023)

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Installing and Loading Packages
Before you can use a package, you need to:

Install it once (downloads from CRAN to your computer)
Load it in each session (makes its functions available)



View source
# Install once -- you only need to do this one time
install.packages("gapminder")
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load every session -- goes at the top of your .qmd
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)









Tip


Never put install.packages() inside a Quarto document that you intend to render or share. Quarto blocks package installation during rendering for security reasons, so the document will fail to render and throw an error. In addition, even if it were allowed, it would attempt to reinstall the package every time the document is rendered, which can create unnecessary delays and compatibility issues in other users’ environments.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Import with readr
readr is the tidyverse package for importing rectangular data files.



Function
File type




read_csv("file.csv")
Comma-separated values


read_tsv("file.tsv")
Tab-separated values


read_delim("file.txt", delim = ";")
Any delimiter






View source
# Import a CSV file and assign it to an object
my_data <- read_csv("data/my_dataset.csv")




For other formats:


View source
library(haven)
my_data <- read_dta("data/dataset.dta") # Stata
my_data <- read_sav("data/dataset.sav") # SPSS

library(readxl)
my_data <- read_excel("data/dataset.xlsx") # Excel





Always assign the result to an object. read_csv("file.csv") alone reads the file but immediately discards it.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Data Export
To save a data frame to disk:


View source
# Write to CSV
write_csv(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.csv")

# Write to RDS (R's native format -- preserves types exactly)
saveRDS(my_data, "output/my_dataset_clean.rds")

# Read it back
my_data <- readRDS("output/my_dataset_clean.rds")











Format
Pros
Cons




.csv
Universal, opens in Excel
Loses type info (factors become strings)


.rds
Preserves all R types exactly
Only readable in R


.dta
Stata-compatible

section: dplyr – A Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
dplyr – A Grammar of Data Manipulation


All dplyr functions follow the same pattern:

First argument: the data frame (automatically filled if working with pipe |>)
Subsequent arguments: what to do
Return value: always a (new) data frame


The core verbs:



Verb
What it does




filter()
Keep rows that meet a condition


select()
Keep or drop columns


mutate()
Create or modify variables


arrange()
Sort rows


summarise()
Compute summary statistics


group_by()
Group rows for grouped operations


ungroup()
Remove grouping

title: QMIR 2026
section: Putting It Together – A Full Pipeline

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Bad Practice vs. Good Practice


Bad practice – nested or intermediate objects:


View source
gap_2007 <- filter(gapminder, year == 2007)
gap_gdp <- mutate(gap_2007, gdp = pop * gdpPercap)
gap_grp <- group_by(gap_gdp, continent)
summarise(gap_grp, mean_gdp = mean(gdp))



Good practice – piped chain:


View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  mutate(gdp = pop * gdpPercap) |>
  group_by(continent) |>
  summarise(mean_gdp = mean(gdp))




Both produce the same result. The piped version reads like a sentence and requires no intermediate objects. However, you should always be aware of what each individual pipe step does. Especially when learning, test each step separately in the console to make sure you understand how the data changes at every stage.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
The Grammar of Graphics
ggplot2 builds plots from layers. Every plot starts the same way:
ggplot(data = <DATA>, aes(x = <VAR>, y = <VAR>, ...)) +
  geom_*()

The three essential pieces:

ggplot() – sets up the canvas and declares the data
aes() – maps variables to visual properties (x, y, colour, size, …)
geom_*() – the geometric object that draws the data (points, lines, bars, …)




Note the + operator, not the pipe |>. ggplot2 layers are added with +.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
A First Scatterplot




View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp,
    color = continent
  )) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (USD)",
    y = "Life Expectancy (years)",
    color = "Continent"
  )








Figure 1: GDP per capita vs. life expectancy, 2007






The plot in Figure 1 shows the classic wealth-health relationship: richer countries tend to have longer life expectancies.
Notice:

data piped into ggplot()
aes() maps variables to visual properties
geom_point() adds the points layer
theme_bw() gives a clean look
labs() adds axis labels

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Log Scale and facet_wrap()




View source
gapminder |>
  filter(year == 2007) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = gdpPercap,
    y = lifeExp
  )) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  facet_wrap(~continent) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Life Expectancy"
  )








Figure 2: GDP vs. life expectancy by continent, 2007






scale_x_log10() – log-transforms the x axis, making the skewed GDP distribution easier to read.
facet_wrap(~ continent) – creates one small panel per continent.
We will go much deeper on ggplot2 next week. Today you are just seeing the shape of it.

title: QMIR 2026
section: Execution Options – Global vs. Cell Level
text:
Execution Options – Global vs. Cell Level
Last week you saw echo and eval as cell-level options. You can also set them globally in the YAML header so they apply to all cells at once:
execute:
  eval: true    # run all code cells
  echo: true    # show all source code
  warning: false  # suppress warnings (e.g. tidyverse startup)
  message: false  # suppress messages
  error: false  # stop on errors (set true to render despite errors)
  freeze: true  # don't re-run cells unless source changes

Cell-level options override global ones for that specific cell:
```{r}
#| echo: false   # overrides global echo: true for this cell only
my_plot
```

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Cell Options Reference








Option
Values
What it controls




eval
true / false
Whether the code is executed


echo
true / false
Whether the source code is shown


warning
true / false
Whether warnings appear in output


message
true / false
Whether messages appear in output


error
true / false
Whether errors stop rendering


include
true / false
Whether cell output appears at all


label
fig-* / tbl-* / any string
Cell identifier for cross-referencing


fig-cap
string
Caption for figure output

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Labels and Cross-References
Labelling a code cell that produces a figure enables automatic cross-references in your prose:


```{r}
#| label: fig-lifeexp
#| fig-cap: "Life expectancy over time"
#| message: false

gapminder |>
  group_by(year, continent) |>
  summarise(mean_le = mean(lifeExp),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_le,
             color = continent)) +
  geom_line() +
  theme_bw()
```

As shown in @fig-lifeexp, life
expectancy has risen across all
continents since 1952.


The label must start with fig- for figures, tbl- for tables
@fig-lifeexp in prose becomes “Figure 1” (auto-numbered) in the rendered output
Figure numbers update automatically if you add or reorder figures
Works in HTML, PDF, and Word output

This is how academic papers keep figure references consistent. Your future self will thank you.

title: QMIR 2026
text:
Suppressing Tidyverse Messages
When you load tidyverse, R prints a message listing attached packages. When you use read_csv(), it prints column types. These are useful interactively, but clutter your rendered document.

Suppress them with cell options or globally:


Cell level:
```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)
```

Global YAML (applies to all cells):
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false



Prefer the global approach in homework submissions so your rendered PDF looks clean.
